title: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Transmission Pathways of Rabies Virus
items.content: Rabies virus is primarily transmitted through bite exposures from infected animals. While most human cases are due to dog bites in regions with endemic canine rabies, non-bite transmissions like aerosols and organ transplants have been documented but are rare.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs mainly via animal bites, especially dogs in areas with endemic canine rabies.
Non-bite exposures include laboratory aerosols, corneal transplantation, solid-organ transplantation, and vascular conduits.
Human-to-human transmission is extremely rare.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Global Epidemiology of Rabies
items.content: Rabies affects a wide range of mammals globally, excluding Antarctica. It causes approximately 59,000 human deaths annually, predominantly in Asia and Africa due to endemic canine rabies.
keyPoints: Occurs worldwide except in Antarctica.
Endemic canine rabies leads to an estimated 59,000 human deaths annually.
Most deaths occur in Asia and Africa; rural populations and children are disproportionately affected.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Control Measures for Rabies in Wildlife
items.content: In North America, rabies control efforts have focused on wildlife reservoirs like bats, raccoons, skunks, and foxes. Vaccination baits are used to manage epizootics in red foxes across Canada and Europe.
keyPoints: Rabies is endemic in wildlife such as bats, raccoons, skunks, and foxes.
Control measures include vaccination baits for red foxes in Canada and Europe.
In the U.S., rabies cases are mainly from bat transmissions.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Rabies Virus Spread and Clinical Implications
items.content: Rabies virus spreads from the site of inoculation to the CNS via retrograde fast axonal transport, reaching a rate of up to approximately 250 mm/day. This rapid spread results in clinical disease after an incubation period typically ranging from 20–90 days. Once established in the CNS, the virus disseminates rapidly across neuroanatomic connections.
keyPoints: Incubation period: usually 20–90 days; rare cases as short as a few days or >1 year.
Virus spread rate via retrograde fast axonal transport: up to ~250 mm/day.
Delays at synapses during CNS entry: approximately every 12 hours.
Clinical implication: Rapid CNS dissemination contributes to the severity of rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Negri Bodies and Neuronal Dysfunction in Rabies
items.content: The presence of Negri bodies, eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions found in a minority of infected neurons, is the most characteristic pathologic finding in rabies. These are commonly observed in Purkinje cells of the cerebellum and pyramidal neurons of the hippocampus. Despite severe clinical symptoms, there is minimal neuronal death, suggesting that dysfunction rather than death causes disease manifestations.
keyPoints: Negri bodies: Eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions composed of rabies virus proteins and RNA.
Commonly observed in Purkinje cells (cerebellum) and pyramidal neurons (hippocampus).
Neuronal death is minimal; dysfunction, possibly due to oxidative stress from mitochondrial dysfunction, leads to clinical symptoms.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Recognizing Rabies: Prodromal Features and Early Neurologic Symptoms
items.content: Rabies should be suspected based on clinical presentation, especially when nonspecific prodromal symptoms like fever, malaise, headache, nausea, vomiting, anxiety, or agitation are followed by specific neurologic symptoms. Paresthesias, pain, or pruritus near the exposure site occur in 50–80% of patients and strongly suggest rabies.
keyPoints: Prodromal features include fever, malaise, headache, nausea, vomiting, anxiety, agitation.
Specific neurologic symptoms: paresthesias, pain, or pruritus near the exposure site occur in 50–80% of patients.
These symptoms suggest rabies and reflect infection with inflammatory changes in local dorsal root or cranial sensory ganglia.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Distinguishing Encephalitic Rabies: Key Features and Progression
items.content: Encephalitic rabies, seen in 80% of cases, presents with fever, confusion, hallucinations, combativeness, seizures, and autonomic dysfunction. Early brainstem involvement leads to hydrophobia and aerophobia due to exaggerated defense reflexes. Episodes of hyperexcitability are followed by periods of lucidity that shorten as the disease progresses.
keyPoints: Encephalitic rabies occurs in 80% of cases.
Symptoms include fever, confusion, hallucinations, combativeness, seizures, hypersalivation, gooseflesh, cardiac arrhythmia, priapism.
Hydrophobia and aerophobia result from brainstem involvement causing exaggerated defense reflexes.
Progression leads to coma and death unless prolonged by supportive measures.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Rabies Diagnosis: Importance of Alternative Testing and Imaging
items.content: In suspected rabies cases, routine laboratory tests often yield normal or nonspecific results. However, mild mononuclear-cell pleocytosis in CSF with mildly elevated protein levels may be observed. Severe pleocytosis (>1000 white cells/μL) should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses. Imaging such as CT scans is typically normal, while MRI may show variable signal abnormalities. These findings necessitate further rabies-specific testing to confirm diagnosis.
keyPoints: Routine tests often yield normal or nonspecific results.
Mild mononuclear-cell pleocytosis and mildly elevated protein levels in CSF are common.
Severe pleocytosis (>1000 white cells/μL) suggests alternative diagnoses.
CT scans usually normal; MRI may show variable signal abnormalities.
Rabies-specific tests should be performed for confirmation.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Techniques for Rabies Confirmation
items.content: For confirming rabies, several diagnostic specimens are useful: serum, CSF, fresh saliva, skin biopsy from the neck, and brain tissue. RT-PCR is highly sensitive and specific for detecting rabies virus RNA in various samples. DFA testing identifies rabies virus antigen in tissues like skin biopsies and brain tissue with high sensitivity and specificity.
keyPoints: Useful specimens: serum, CSF, fresh saliva, neck skin biopsy, brain tissue.
RT-PCR detects rabies virus RNA; highly sensitive and specific.
DFA testing identifies rabies virus antigen in tissues.
Rabies virus-specific antibodies may not develop until late in the disease.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differentiating Rabies from Other Encephalitides and Psychiatric Disorders
items.content: Rabies can be challenging to diagnose without a known animal exposure, especially when presenting with neuropsychiatric symptoms. Unlike acute viral encephalitis (e.g., herpes simplex or arboviral), rabies often presents with early neurologic symptoms at the bite site and brainstem involvement while preserving consciousness. It may also mimic psychiatric disorders due to unusual neuropsychiatric presentations.
keyPoints: Rabies presentation differs from acute viral encephalitis by having early neurologic symptoms at the bite site and brainstem involvement with preserved consciousness.
Rabies can be misdiagnosed as a psychiatric disorder due to its unusual neuropsychiatric symptoms.
Rabies hysteria, characterized by aggressive behavior and inability to communicate, has a shorter incubation period than rabies itself.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.title: Distinguishing Paralytic Rabies from Guillain-Barré Syndrome
items.content: Paralytic rabies can be confused with Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS). Key differentiating features include the presence of fever, bladder dysfunction, a normal sensory examination, and CSF pleocytosis in paralytic rabies. Conversely, GBS may follow vaccination with neural tissue-derived rabies vaccines, potentially leading to misdiagnosis as vaccine failure.
keyPoints: Paralytic rabies can mimic Guillain-Barré syndrome but is distinguished by fever, bladder dysfunction, normal sensory examination, and CSF pleocytosis.
Guillain-Barré syndrome may occur post-vaccination with neural tissue-derived rabies vaccines, leading to potential misdiagnosis as paralytic rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.title: Rabies Prognosis and Importance of Early Postexposure Therapy
items.content: Rabies is almost uniformly fatal once symptoms appear, but it can be nearly always prevented with timely postexposure prophylaxis (PEP). Most survivors had received rabies vaccine before the onset of disease or had neutralizing antibodies in their CSF.
keyPoints: Rabies is nearly always preventable with appropriate PEP during early incubation.
All but 2 of 33 documented survivors received rabies vaccine pre-onset or had neutralizing antibodies in CSF.
Most patients die within days of illness onset despite aggressive care.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Criteria for Postexposure Prophylaxis (PEP) Initiation
items.content: PEP is crucial after potential rabies exposure. Decisions are based on exposure history and local epidemiology. Healthy dogs, cats, or ferrets can be observed for 10 days; if they remain healthy, PEP may not be needed.
keyPoints: PEP should be initiated without waiting for lab results in high-risk exposures or endemic areas.
Healthy dogs, cats, or ferrets can be observed for 10 days before deciding on PEP necessity.
If an animal develops rabies signs during observation, it must be euthanized and tested.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Components of Effective Postexposure Prophylaxis (PEP)
items.content: Effective PEP includes local wound care and both active and passive immunization. Local wound care is crucial, even if vaccine initiation is delayed. Previously unvaccinated individuals should receive rabies immune globulin (RIG) as soon as possible.
keyPoints: PEP involves local wound care and active/passive immunization.
Wound care should not be delayed; all bites/wounds washed with soap and water.
Previously unvaccinated persons need RIG, ideally administered no later than 7 days after the first vaccine dose.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which family does the rabies virus belong to?
items.explanation: The rabies virus is a member of the family Rhabdoviridae.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What type of genome does the rabies virus have?
items.explanation: Rabies virus has a single-strand RNA that is nonsegmented and negative-sense.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which animal reservoirs are associated with endemic rabies in North America?
items.explanation: In North American wildlife reservoirs, rabies is endemic in bats, raccoons, skunks, and foxes.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which region has successfully eliminated endemic canine rabies?
items.explanation: Endemic canine rabies has been eliminated from Latin America due to successful control efforts.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: During the incubation period of rabies, where is the virus primarily thought to reside?
options: At or close to the site of inoculation
items.explanation: During most of the incubation period, rabies virus is thought to be present at or close to the site of inoculation.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is a characteristic pathologic finding in rabies?
items.explanation: The most characteristic pathologic finding in rabies is the Negri body.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: How does rabies virus spread within the peripheral nervous system?
items.explanation: Rabies virus spreads centripetally along peripheral nerves toward the spinal cord or brainstem via retrograde fast axonal transport.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most strongly suggestive of rabies when occurring near the site of exposure?
items.explanation: Paresthesias, pain, or pruritus near the site of exposure strongly suggest rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which form of rabies is characterized by muscle weakness and lacks hyperexcitability, hydrophobia, and aerophobia?
items.explanation: The paralytic form of rabies is characterized by muscle weakness and lacks hyperexcitability, hydrophobia, and aerophobia.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What symptom in encephalitic rabies results from dysfunction of infected brainstem neurons?
items.explanation: Hydrophobia in encephalitic rabies results from dysfunction of infected brainstem neurons.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which imaging technique is most likely to show signal abnormalities in rabies patients?
items.explanation: MRI brain scans may show signal abnormalities in the brainstem or other gray-matter areas, but these findings are variable and nonspecific.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a diagnostically useful specimen for rabies?
items.explanation: Corneal impression smears are of low diagnostic yield and are generally not performed.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which laboratory test is highly sensitive and specific for detecting rabies virus RNA?
items.explanation: Detection of rabies virus RNA by RT-PCR is highly sensitive and specific.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition can present with early neurologic symptoms at the site of a bite and features of brainstem involvement, distinguishing it from acute viral encephalitis?
items.explanation: Rabies can present with early neurologic symptoms at the bite site and brainstem involvement.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.question: What condition may mimic Guillain-Barré syndrome but is distinguished by fever, bladder dysfunction, a normal sensory examination, and CSF pleocytosis?
items.explanation: Paralytic rabies may mimic Guillain-Barré syndrome but has distinguishing features.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.question: Which disorder is characterized by behavioral changes, autonomic instability, hypoventilation, and seizures, especially in young females?
items.explanation: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis presents with these symptoms, especially in young females.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.question: Which group of individuals is most likely to survive rabies without receiving a vaccine?
options: Individuals with neutralizing antibodies to rabies virus in CSF at clinical presentation
Patients treated aggressively in a critical care unit
Survivors of rabies who had not been exposed to an animal bite
items.explanation: The two documented survivors of rabies without vaccine had neutralizing antibodies to rabies virus in CSF.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended course of action if a healthy dog, cat, or ferret bites someone?
options: Observe the animal for 10 days and initiate PEP only if it develops signs of rabies
Euthanize the animal immediately regardless of its health status
Wait for laboratory results before deciding on any action
items.explanation: Healthy dogs, cats, or ferrets may be confined and observed for 10 days; PEP is not necessary if they remain healthy.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What should be done with the head of an animal that develops signs of rabies during observation?
options: Dispose of it in a regular landfill
Transport under refrigeration for laboratory examination
Leave it at the site where the animal was confined
Incinerate immediately to prevent further spread
items.explanation: The head should be transported under refrigeration, and rabies virus sought by DFA testing.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: When is passive immunization with rabies immune globulin (RIG) not immediately necessary?
options: If the animal remains healthy after 10 days of observation
If RIG is available but delayed beyond 7 days after first vaccine dose
For individuals who have previously been immunized against rabies
When a person has had contact with an animal in an area without endemic rabies
items.explanation: Passive immunization is not needed for those who have previously been immunized.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Human-to-human transmission of rabies is common.
items.explanation: Human-to-human transmission of rabies is extremely rare.

items.subtopic: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Most human rabies cases in North America are transmitted by bats.
items.explanation: In North America, indigenously acquired human rabies is usually associated with transmission from bats.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: There is well-documented evidence for hematogenous spread of rabies virus.
items.explanation: There is no well-documented evidence for hematogenous spread of rabies virus.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Negri bodies are observed in all cases of rabies.
items.explanation: Negri bodies are not observed in all cases of rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: The prodromal phase of rabies is characterized by specific neurologic symptoms such as paresthesias near the site of exposure.
items.explanation: Paresthesias near the site of exposure are early specific neurologic symptoms in the prodromal phase.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Patients with paralytic rabies typically present with hyperexcitability and hydrophobia.
items.explanation: Paralytic rabies lacks the cardinal features of encephalitic rabies, including hyperexcitability and hydrophobia.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Negative antemortem rabies-specific laboratory tests exclude a diagnosis of rabies.
items.explanation: Negative antemortem rabies-specific laboratory tests never exclude a diagnosis of rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Serum neutralizing antibodies to rabies virus are always present in previously unimmunized patients.
items.explanation: Some patients die without detectable serum neutralizing antibodies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Rabies hysteria is characterized by a longer incubation period than rabies.
items.explanation: Rabies hysteria has a shorter incubation period than rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.statement: No antiviral therapy has demonstrated efficacy in the treatment of rabies.
items.explanation: There is no established effective antiviral therapy for rabies.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.statement: Rabies can be effectively treated once symptoms appear.
items.explanation: There is no effective therapy for rabies after the onset of symptoms.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Local wound care should not be delayed even if immunization is postponed.
items.explanation: Wound care should not be delayed, as it may greatly decrease the risk of rabies virus infection.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Rabies virus variants can be identified by reverse-transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR).
items.reason: These techniques are helpful in human cases with no known history of exposure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why RT-PCR is used.

items.assertion: Transmission of rabies through aerosols in caves has been common.
items.reason: Aerosols generated in caves containing millions of Brazilian free-tail bats have rarely caused human rabies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly states that such transmission is rare.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections | RABIES | ETIOLOGIC AGENT | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Rabies may be misdiagnosed as a psychiatric disorder.
items.reason: Rabies can present with unusual neuropsychiatric symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Rabies

items.assertion: PEP is initiated without waiting for laboratory results in high-risk exposures.
items.reason: In areas where canine rabies is endemic, immediate prophylaxis is crucial to prevent the disease.
items.explanation: Immediate PEP is recommended in high-risk situations and endemic areas.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Passive immunization with RIG should be administered after day 7 of the first vaccine dose.
items.reason: Endogenous antibodies are being produced, making passive immunization counterproductive.
items.explanation: RIG is not recommended after day 7 as it may interfere with endogenous antibody production.
items.reference: Rabies and Other Rhabdovirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION
